coredata.dc:title: Etching single-wall carbon nanotubes into green and yellow single-layer graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yongqiang
Pang, Hongchang
Ren, Shuyan
Chen, Congqiang
Chi, Yuwu
Yu, Ting
coredata.dc:description: Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) have been used to prepare single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through a simple and green hydrothermal etching method. After the characterization of products and intermediates with scanning electronic microscopy, Raman and FTIR, a possible mechanism has been proposed for the formation of GQDs. The treatment of SWCNTs has resulted in two kinds of GQDs (i.e. GQD1 and GQD2), both of which are monodisperse and single-layered nanosheets with an average lateral dimension of 8nm and an average height of 0.5nm. Excited with 365nm UV light, aqueous solutions of GQDs1 and GQDs2 give green and yellow luminescence, respectively. The differences in optical property between GQDs1 and GQDs2 mainly results from their differences in degree of oxidation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of SWCNTs (a), O-SWCNTs (b), R-O-SWCNTs (c), and the sediment after the R-O-SWCNTs were oxidized with 8M HNO3 (d).
FTIR (a) and Raman (b) spectra of SWCNTs, O-SWCNTs, R-O-SWCNTs.
XPS survey and high resolution XPS spectra of C1s for GQDs1 and GQDs2.
TEM images of GQDs1 (a) and GQDs2 (b). The insets in (a) and (b) are the corresponding width distribution.

AFM images of GQDs1 (a) and GQDs2 (b). The insets below (a) and (b) are the corresponding height profile along the red lines. The insets in (a) and (b) are the corresponding height distribution. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra of GQDs1 and GQDs2.

UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra (recorded for progressively longer excitation wavelengths in 20nm increments) of GQDs1 and GQDs2 in water solution. Inset: Optical photographs of GQD solutions under visible light (left) and a UV beam of 365nm (right).
Diagram for the preparation of GQDs1 and GQDs2 from SWCNTs.
Elemental analysis results of the SWCNTs, O-SWCNTs, R-O-SWCNTs, GQDs1 and GQDs2.

head.ce:title: Etching single-wall carbon nanotubes into green and yellow single-layer graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, Department of Chemistry, Fuzhou Univiersity, Fujian 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, National University of Singapore, Singapore 117542, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) have been used to prepare single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through a simple and green hydrothermal etching method. After the characterization of products and intermediates with scanning electronic microscopy, Raman and FTIR, a possible mechanism has been proposed for the formation of GQDs. The treatment of SWCNTs has resulted in two kinds of GQDs (i.e. GQD1 and GQD2), both of which are monodisperse and single-layered nanosheets with an average lateral dimension of 8nm and an average height of 0.5nm. Excited with 365nm UV light, aqueous solutions of GQDs1 and GQDs2 give green and yellow luminescence, respectively. The differences in optical property between GQDs1 and GQDs2 mainly results from their differences in degree of oxidation.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a kind of emerging carbon-based luminescent materials, have attracted more and more attention. GQDs are usually graphene nano-sheets of less than 100nm in lateral dimension, exhibiting unique optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects [1]. Compared with traditional semiconductor-based quantum dots (QDs), GQDs show many advantages, such as low toxicity, robust chemical inertness and ease of fabrication [2]. As a result, GQDs are considered as promising candidates to replace those traditional QDs, and have been applied in many fields, such as bioimaging [3], sensing [4], catalysts and photovoltaic devices [5–7]. Therefore, preparing high quality GQDs is of particular significance. So far, some methods have been developed to prepare GQDs using different raw materials, including graphite crystallites [1,8], fullerenes [9], graphite nanofiber [10], XC-72 carbon black [11], multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [8], graphene nano-sheets [6,12–14], and some special organics [15,16]. However, most of the developed methods are not satisfied in some aspects. For example, the methods of carving graphite crystallites by high-resolution electron-beam lithography and cage-opening fullerenes on the ruthenium surfaces are limited by the low productivity and high cost [1,9]. The most popular methods of cutting graphene nano-sheets through hydrothermal [12,13], solvothermal treatment [17], ultrasonic shearing [18], and electrochemical oxidation [14], and the methods of carbonizing some special organic precursors through stepwise synthesis [6], pyrolysis [15], and pyrolysis combined with chemical oxidation [16] are usually difficult to obtain single-layer GQDs with uniform size distribution. Therefore, finding new precursors to prepare GQDs with good shapes and properties is of considerable research interest. Single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) should be ideal raw materials to prepare single-layer graphene-based nanomaterials due to their special structural features. For example, SWCNTs can be longitudinally unzipped into graphene nanoribbons [19]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no attention has been paid to cutting SWCNTs into GQDs. Herein, we report the preparation of GQDs from SWCNTs by a green and simple hydrothermally etching method. SWCNTs were firstly oxidized with 8M HNO3, followed with a hydrothermal treatment. As a result, the tubular structure of SWCNTs was almost destroyed, forming abundant of graphene nanosheets. The graphene nanosheets were successively released into solution with 8M HNO3 and 12M HNO3. Finally, two kinds of water-soluble and single-layered GQDs with different degree of oxidation were obtained.

SWCNTs (90%) with an average diameter less than 2nm and a length of 5–15μm were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port Co., Ltd., China. Other chemicals were analytically pure and used as received. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

High resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were recorded on an electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200KV). The height of the obtained GQDs was characterized by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Nanoman, Veeco, Santa Barbara, CA) by using tapping mode. Scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a Field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, JSM-6700F). Elemental analysis was carried out using an organic elemental analyzer (Vario MICRO). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data for the GQD solutions deposited on glass substrates were measured by an ESCAlab 250 XPS system having an Al K source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Raman spectra were measured using a Renishaw 1000 microspectrometer (excitation wavelength of 514.5nm). All fluorescence spectra were obtained by a spectrofluorometer (Fluoromax-4). The infrared (IR) spectrum was obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet 360). UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda 750).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and formation mechanism of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Secondly, the obtained O-SWCNTs were dispersed in 20mL DI water, and heated hydrothermally in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 12h with a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1. After that, the obtained suspension was centrifuged at 2770g for 10min to collect the deposit (labelled as reduced O-SWCNTs, R-O-SWCNTs). SEM images indicate that the hydrothermal treatment makes the morphology of O-SWCNTs change greatly. The tubular structure of O-SWCNTs was almost destroyed, forming a lot of aggregated nano-sheets (Fig. 1c). Besides the change in morphology, the hydrothermal treatment can remove some oxygen-containing groups from O-SWCNTs. The oxygen content decreases from 15.53% to 11.12% (Table 1), and the relative IR absorption intensities of both C–O and CO groups decrease dramatically when compared with that of CC group (Fig. 2a). In other words, O-SWCNTs were reduced in the hydrothermal treatment. This result is similar with that of hydrothermally treating graphene oxide [20]. Moreover, the hydrothermal treatment decreases the disorder structures obviously, i.e. the ID /I
G value of R-O-SWCNTs decreases from ∼0.82 to ∼0.55 (Fig. 2b).

Based on the above mentioned results of elemental analysis, FTIR and Raman spectra, a possible mechanism can be proposed here for the formation of the graphene based nano-sheets. SWCNTs were initially oxidized to O-SWCNTs in the chemical oxidation treatment, producing a lot of oxygen-containing groups and unstable disorder structures. The subsequent hydrothermal treatment provided an environment of high temperature and high pressure. As a result, the tubular structure of O-SWCNTs were destroyed due to the oxygen-containing groups were partially removed. Finally, under the continuous high temperature and high pressure condition, the reactive disorder structures reacted with each other, forming a lot of nanosheets.Thirdly, the collected R-O-SWCNTs from the second step were further oxidized with concentrated HNO3. As mentioned above, a lot of graphene based nanosheets have been formed but fixed in the R-O-SWCNTs matrixes. Chemical oxidation is an effective method to release GQDs from their aggregates into solution by introducing oxygen-containing groups [11,16]. Therefore, a lot of GQDs were released into the solution after R-O-SWCNTs were refluxed with 8M HNO3 for 24h. The suspension was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 2770g for 10min to obtain yellow supernatant and black sediment. On one hand, the supernatant was heated at 200°C to evaporate the water and nitric acid till a reddish-brown solid (ca. 0.023g, 11.5%) was finally obtained. The obtained reddish-brown solid was labelled as GQDs1. On the other hand, the black sediment was collected because abundant graphene nano-sheets were still contained in it (Fig. 1d). The sediment was further refluxed with 50mL of 12M HNO3 for 24h followed by cooling, centrifugation and drying to obtain another reddish-brown solid (ca. 0.031g, 16.5%), labelled as GQDs2. It should be noted here, the use of the concentrated HNO3 should be careful for its highly corrosive property.

A three-step method has been developed to prepare single-layer GQDs from SWCNTs. Firstly, SWCNTs were oxidized into O-SWCNTs with 8M HNO3. Subsequently, the obtained O-SWCNTs were heated hydrothermally to 200°C, resulting in the destruction of the tubal structure of SWCNTs and the formation of abundant graphene-based nanosheets. Finally, two kinds of GQDs (GQDs1 and GQDs2) were obtained after the graphene based nanosheets were released by 8M and 12M HNO3, respectively. GQDs1 and GQDs2 are both mono-dispersive and single-layered nanosheets with an average lateral size of 8nm and an average height of 0.5nm, but show different optical properties mainly due to different degree of oxidation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21075018), Program for New Century Excellent Talents in Chinese University (NCET-10-0019),National Basic Research Program of China (2010CB732400), and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116).
